headlineSummary: Anthropic had the top positive signal on broad mixed evidence, while OpenAI and Copilot were negative on enterprise-access and coding-tool complaints [E1][E2][E5][E6][E7].

ranking.summary: Microsoft Copilot was negative on limited evidence, with commenters calling it the weakest mainstream coding offering and blaming Microsoft prioritization for decline [E6][E7].
OpenAI was negative overall, driven by complaints about poor Azure/corporate access and low trust in enterprise settings, partially offset by Bedrock-based access as a compliance unlock [E1][E2][E3].
Anthropic had a slight positive signal on broad mixed evidence: some praised Bedrock and trusted-access adoption, but others criticized Claude Code for harming code quality and teamwork [E1][E2][E5].

evidence.excerpt: Availability through Bedrock has been a major driver in use of Anthropic in my org. And I am betting there is actual margin in it as well. I wonder if this is directly linked to the split up with Microsoft. Just from my anecdata, OpenAI is getting completely ignored in serious enterprise deployments because what they offer on Azure sucks and there is no other corporate friendly way to get it. They probably saw themselves getting destroyed in enterprise and realised it was existential to be able to compete with Anthropic on AWS.

annotations.rationale: Says Bedrock availability has been a major driver of Anthropic usage in the commenter’s organization.
States OpenAI is being ignored in enterprise because its Azure offering 'sucks' and lacks a corporate-friendly access path.

evidence.excerpt: Claude got a looooot more buy in with a lot of privacy-concerned orgs I work with because they could access it through their "trusted" intermediate Amazon. OpenAI has been banned and is not trusted. I'm not sure that I agree with these orgs' legal teams' assessments, but they definitely read the terms of service far closer than I did. We will see if this changes the equation, but it feels like OpenAI is pretty far behind and playing catch up on all fronts. Though to be honest, "pretty far behind" is like 2-8 weeks in the AI world, so it may not matter a ton, it's mostly perception. And for me and my information bubble, perception of OpenAI is rock-bottom due to Sam Altman. From appearing unethical to appearing unhinged with demands from fabs and everything else, I'm not a fan.

annotations.rationale: Says Claude got much more buy-in from privacy-concerned organizations because access through Amazon felt trusted.
Says OpenAI is banned, not trusted, behind on all fronts, and has rock-bottom perception due to Sam Altman.

evidence.excerpt: OpenAI on Bedrock basically lets those orgs skip the separate DPA negotiation with OpenAI. Could be a bigger unlock than it looks on paper.

annotations.rationale: Frames Bedrock access to OpenAI as a meaningful compliance/privacy unlock by avoiding a separate DPA negotiation.

evidence.excerpt: Who owns the code Claude Code wrote?

annotations.rationale: The title is a legal/IP question about Claude Code without clear praise or criticism.

evidence.excerpt: I think that the gold rush approach happening right now around me (my company EMs forcing me to work with claude as fast as possible) show really short-sight of all the management people. First - I lose my understanding of the code base by relying too much on claude code. Second - we drop all the good coding practices (like XP, code review etc.) because claude is reviewing claude's code. Third - we just take a big smelly dump on the teamwork

annotations.rationale: Strongly criticizes Claude/Claude Code as harming understanding, coding practices, and teamwork.

evidence.excerpt: Copilot, though, has been consistently the weakest mainstream AI coding offering. Inferior to Cursor or Windsurf at editor completions, inferior to Codex, Claude, OpenCode, blah blah blah, at agentic coding and also the old-school chat-style... And now, it's no longer cheap AND now sucks even more than it has all along — the new $39/month plan is not only worse than all its competitors, but worse than its own $10 plan was a month ago — by a lot.

annotations.rationale: Directly calls Copilot the weakest mainstream coding offering and says the new plan is worse and more expensive.
Says Copilot is inferior to Codex, implying Codex is better on coding tasks.
Says Copilot is inferior to Claude, implying Claude is better on coding tasks.

evidence.excerpt: the switch from being independent to being part of Microsoft, having resources pushed to Copilot instead of core service, the organization structure itself, a reliance on vibe coding, etc etc.

annotations.rationale: Criticizes prioritizing Copilot over GitHub’s core service as part of GitHub’s decline.
